metadata.title: The Resume That Disappeared: Why Your Best-Looking CV Scores Zero
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Reallocation Engine candidate 05. Source chapter: the-reallocation-engine/chapters/13-resumes-that-survive-the-filter.md. Mechanism: ATS parsers read in document order — two-column layouts interleave into nonsense, graphics produce no text, the polished resume vanishes before a human sees it.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = single-column / parseable / visible to the parser; CRIMSON = two-column / graphic / invisible to the parser. GOLD = editor's-pen highlight, once. Never swap mid-film.

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, ink serif type, teal = parseable/safe, crimson = unreadable/invisible, gold highlighter as the single editor-pen voice

metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO comparison of ATS vendors; NO discussion of what makes bullet content compelling to humans (the second stage, mentioned but not developed); NO Playwright/Chromium rendering details. Two accents: TEAL (parseable/safe) and CRIMSON (unreadable/invisible). One STILL slot for the cold-open hook image.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: She spent a weekend on it. Two columns, skill bars, her name set large in a header graphic. The best-looking resume she had ever made. She submitted it to forty companies.
beats.scene_description: a beautifully designed two-column resume on a cream desk, tasteful sidebar skill bars, name in a prominent header banner, polished and professional, viewed from slightly above, slow push-in on the header name — before the reveal of what a parser sees
beats.image_prompt: B01, a beautifully designed two-column resume document lying on an aged newsprint desk surface, name displayed in a large elegant header banner at the top, sidebar with tasteful skill bar graphics, professional and polished, editorial collage style, desaturated and flat print reproduction, warm cream tones, viewed from 30-degree overhead angle, soft diffuse light from upper left, no shadows, no glows — do NOT show a computer screen, a person, or text legible enough to read
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] the beautiful resume — hook plate, push-in on the header name

beats.narration_text: Most rejected her before a human opened it. Not because the content was weak. Because the first reader wasn't a human.

card.copy: 82% of companies screen resumes with software first.
card.sub: ~1 in 5 applicants are auto-rejected before any human review

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] screening stat — the machine reads first

beats.narration_text: A visually impressive resume should reach human reviewers. Here is the case where the most polished CV gets auto-rejected before any human sees it. Why?

card.copy: The most polished CV gets auto-rejected before any human sees it.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION — gap formula on screen

beats.narration_text: There's a fork before any human judgment. The parser reads first. If it extracts clean text — name, title, dates — the application reaches human review. If it cannot, the system auto-rejects. No human ever opens it.

production_viz.label: the fork before the human
production_viz.mechanic: a resume icon at left; one path goes teal (parser reads cleanly -> human review -> interview), one path goes crimson (parser sees noise -> auto-reject -> no human); the fork happens before any human judgment
production_viz.note: fork diagram; no verdict yet on mechanism — just establish the two outcomes

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] fork diagram — parser reads first, two outcomes

beats.narration_text: The parser reads text in document order — left to right across the full page width. In a two-column layout, the left column's first line and the right column's first line sit at the same vertical position. The parser takes them together. Left and right interleave, line by line, into incoherent text.

production_viz.label: two columns -> interleaved nonsense
production_viz.mechanic: two-column layout shown; arrows sweep left-to-right across both columns together, one row at a time, showing extracted text appearing as alternating fragments from each column — the resulting 'extracted text' block is crimson and garbled
production_viz.note: the core mechanism — document-order reading of columns; crimson = the broken output

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] document-order scan of two columns -> interleaved text

beats.narration_text: Graphics are the second failure. A name set in a header banner image is invisible to the parser. Literally absent. The parser cannot do optical character recognition — it reads embedded text only. If your name is an image, the parser doesn't know your name.

production_viz.label: graphic elements -> parser sees nothing
production_viz.mechanic: a header banner region with a name shown; a parser scanner beam sweeps over it and extracts nothing — the output slot beneath stays blank/crimson; then skill bars shown, scanner sweeps, output slot blank; both image elements produce zero text
production_viz.note: establish the two graphic failure modes: name-in-banner and skill bars

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] parser scans header graphic and skill bars -> blank output

beats.narration_text: The safe structures are their opposites. Single-column flow. Real text characters for every piece of content that needs extracting. Standard headings as plain words. Dates adjacent to the roles they describe. These constraints don't prevent a good resume. They ensure it's actually read.

production_viz.label: safe zones vs danger zones
production_viz.mechanic: two-column visual: left side (TEAL) lists safe structures — single column, text headings, dates as plain text, skills as list; right side (CRIMSON) lists danger zones — two-column, header graphic, skill bars, tables for layout; labels emerge one by one
production_viz.note: the quick-reference visual; teal = safe, crimson = danger

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] safe zones (teal) vs danger zones (crimson) reference

beats.narration_text: Ayesha's two-column PDF. The parser extracts 'Data Analyst, 2023-2024' mid-sentence between two unrelated bullets from opposite columns. Her name, set in a header banner graphic: absent. The skills sidebar with labeled bars: zero text. The same content in single-column format: name on line one, each title adjacent to its date, skills as a plain list.

production_viz.label: paste-test: designed vs parsed
production_viz.mechanic: side by side: left (what the resume looks like — clean, designed) vs right (what the parser extracted — interleaved fragments, [NAME ABSENT], [SKILLS: NONE], a red garbled wall); then transform to show single-column version produces clean parse output in teal
production_viz.note: the key visual object from the card; manim move: transform

beats.narration_text: Same resume, same content, one renders invisible. The designed version is better-looking — for a reader it will never reach. The single-column version passes the parser. Then it says something true to the human behind it.

props.headline: Same resume, same content, one renders invisible. The designed version is…

beats.scene_description: two resume documents side by side on a newsprint surface — left: the polished two-column version with a large X mark or crimson overlay indicating rejection; right: a simple clean single-column version with a teal checkmark or teal border indicating it passed; editorial collage style
beats.image_prompt: B09, two resume documents lying on aged newsprint side by side, left document is beautifully designed with two columns and header graphic marked with a crimson rejection X, right document is a plain single-column resume marked with a teal approval checkmark, editorial collage style, desaturated flat print reproduction, warm cream tones, top-down view, soft even light, no shadows, no glows, no people, no legible text
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] two resumes side by side — rejected vs approved

beats.narration_text: A resume a human would admire and a machine cannot read is a resume no human will ever see. The paste test is the check: copy all text from the PDF and paste it. What you see is what the parser sees.

production_viz.label: the paste test rule
production_viz.mechanic: a simple serif quote card with the paste-test principle; gold highlighter sweeps the key phrase 'what you see is what the parser sees'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] paste-test rule card with gold highlight

beats.narration_text: Beautiful design disappears. Parseable layout reaches the human. The resume's job is to pass the parser first, then say something true.

card.copy: Beautiful design disappears. Parseable layout reaches the human.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the The Reallocation Engine series.
